weaknesses.title: Improper Authentication
weaknesses.description: The software does not sufficiently verify that a claimed identity is actually the identity it claims to be. When authentication is not performed or is performed incorrectly, an attacker may be able to gain access to resources or functionality that should be restricted. This root weakness underlies nearly every authentication vulnerability and manifests in many forms including missing authentication, authentication bypass, and weak authentication schemes. It is the parent class for most authentication-related CWEs.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review all authentication entry points to verify that every critical function requires authentication. Use SAST rules to detect missing authentication annotations or middleware on API endpoints. Search for authentication bypass patterns where fallback logic grants access on error. Run dynamic testing to confirm that unauthenticated requests to protected resources return 401/403. Check for authentication state confusion in multi-step flows.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement a centralized authentication framework that is applied uniformly across all application entry points. Use well-tested authentication libraries and frameworks rather than custom implementations. Enforce multi-factor authentication for sensitive operations. Ensure all API endpoints are protected by authentication middleware by default, requiring explicit opt-out for public endpoints. Conduct regular authentication architecture reviews and penetration tests focused on authentication bypass.

weaknesses.title: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
weaknesses.description: The product does not perform any authentication for functionality that requires a provable user identity or consumes a significant amount of resources. This weakness occurs when developers assume that certain functions will only be reached through authenticated pathways, failing to enforce authentication at each critical function boundary. Administrative interfaces, internal APIs, and debugging endpoints are commonly left without authentication, assuming network-level controls provide sufficient protection.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Enumerate all application endpoints and verify each one enforces authentication. Use DAST tools to crawl the application and send unauthenticated requests to every discovered endpoint. Review middleware configuration to ensure authentication filters are applied globally rather than per-route. Search for admin panels, API documentation endpoints, health checks, and debug endpoints that may be unintentionally exposed. Inspect infrastructure for management interfaces accessible without credentials.
weaknesses.remediation: Apply authentication middleware globally to all routes by default, then explicitly whitelist only those endpoints that must be publicly accessible. Use a deny-by-default authentication policy. Implement automated tests that verify all sensitive endpoints reject unauthenticated requests. Integrate DAST scanning in CI/CD to detect endpoints missing authentication. For internal APIs, enforce service-to-service authentication using mutual TLS or signed tokens rather than relying on network segmentation alone.

weaknesses.title: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts
weaknesses.description: The software does not implement sufficient measures to prevent multiple failed authentication attempts within a short time frame, allowing brute force attacks to succeed. Without rate limiting, account lockout, or progressive delays, an attacker can systematically try all possible passwords or credential combinations. This weakness is especially critical for internet-facing authentication endpoints where attackers can leverage distributed botnets and residential proxies to evade IP-based restrictions.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test authentication endpoints by sending 100+ rapid failed login attempts and verifying that lockout or rate limiting engages. Monitor authentication logs for patterns of repeated failures from single IPs or targeting single accounts. Check for the presence of account lockout policies, CAPTCHA challenges, or exponential backoff mechanisms. Verify that rate limiting applies consistently across all authentication pathways including API, mobile, and legacy endpoints. Test whether distributed attacks from multiple IPs are detected.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement multi-layer brute force protection: (1) account lockout after 5-10 failed attempts with configurable lockout duration, (2) IP-based rate limiting using sliding window counters, (3) CAPTCHA challenges after 3 failed attempts, (4) exponential backoff delays. Use adaptive rate limiting that considers request velocity, geographic distribution, and device fingerprinting. Monitor for and alert on distributed brute force patterns. Consider WebAuthn/FIDO2 to eliminate password-based brute force entirely.

weaknesses.title: Use of Single-factor Authentication
weaknesses.description: The system relies on only one authentication factor (typically something the user knows, such as a password) to verify user identity, providing insufficient assurance of identity. Single-factor authentication is vulnerable to credential theft, phishing, brute force, and social engineering attacks. Modern threat landscapes require multi-factor authentication for any system handling sensitive data, administrative functions, or financial transactions. The absence of MFA is the single most exploited authentication weakness in real-world breaches.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Audit authentication flows to determine if MFA is required for all user types and access scenarios. Check IdP configuration to verify MFA policies are enforced rather than optional. Review conditional access policies to ensure MFA is not bypassed for certain network locations or device types. Test whether MFA can be downgraded or skipped through parameter manipulation. Verify MFA is enforced for API access and service accounts, not just interactive logins.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement multi-factor authentication using at least two distinct factors: something you know (password), something you have (hardware token, phone), or something you are (biometric). Prefer phishing-resistant MFA methods such as FIDO2/WebAuthn security keys or platform authenticators. Configure conditional access policies requiring MFA for all users, with stricter requirements for privileged accounts. Implement step-up authentication for sensitive operations even within authenticated sessions. Provide users with backup MFA methods to prevent lockout.

weaknesses.title: Use of Password System for Primary Authentication
weaknesses.description: The system uses a password-based authentication mechanism as its primary and sole authentication method for critical functions where stronger authentication is warranted. Password-based systems are inherently vulnerable to a wide range of attacks including phishing, credential stuffing, brute force, social engineering, and shoulder surfing. For high-assurance applications, passwords alone are insufficient regardless of complexity requirements. NIST SP 800-63B recommends supplementing passwords with additional factors for AAL2 and above.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review authentication architecture documentation to determine the primary authentication mechanism. Check whether the application supports or requires stronger authentication methods such as hardware tokens, biometrics, or certificate-based authentication. Audit the authentication flow for critical functions (financial transactions, admin operations) to verify stronger-than-password authentication is enforced. Review compliance requirements to identify where password-only authentication violates policy.
weaknesses.remediation: Adopt a multi-factor authentication strategy where passwords are supplemented with a second factor. For high-assurance environments, implement passwordless authentication using FIDO2/WebAuthn, certificate-based authentication, or platform authenticators. Use risk-based authentication that dynamically adjusts authentication strength based on the sensitivity of the requested operation. Implement step-up authentication for critical functions. Plan a phased migration to passwordless authentication as the primary method.

weaknesses.title: Weak Password Requirements
weaknesses.description: The product does not require passwords that meet minimum strength criteria, allowing users to set passwords that are easily guessed or cracked. Weak password policies that permit short passwords, common dictionary words, or passwords without sufficient complexity enable brute force and dictionary attacks. However, per NIST SP 800-63B, password complexity rules (requiring uppercase, special characters) are less effective than minimum length requirements and breached password checks. The focus should be on preventing known compromised passwords rather than enforcing arbitrary complexity.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test password creation and change flows with weak passwords (e.g., 'password', '123456', single characters). Verify minimum length requirements of at least 8 characters (12+ recommended). Check if the system rejects passwords found in known breach databases. Test whether common passwords and variations of the username are rejected. Review password policy configuration for alignment with NIST SP 800-63B recommendations.
weaknesses.remediation: Enforce a minimum password length of 12 characters with a maximum of at least 64 characters. Check new passwords against a list of known compromised passwords (HIBP API or downloaded datasets). Do not impose arbitrary complexity rules; instead focus on length and breach detection. Allow all printable Unicode characters in passwords. Implement real-time password strength feedback during creation. Consider adopting passphrase-based approaches. Follow NIST SP 800-63B guidelines for password policy.

weaknesses.title: Insufficiently Protected Credentials
weaknesses.description: The product transmits or stores account credentials using methods that do not provide adequate confidentiality, allowing credentials to be intercepted or retrieved by unauthorized parties. This weakness encompasses multiple failure modes including cleartext credential transmission, weak credential encryption, reversible credential encoding, and inadequate protection of credential storage. It is a parent weakness that encompasses many specific credential protection failures and is frequently identified in security assessments.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Capture and analyze network traffic to detect credentials transmitted in cleartext or with weak encryption. Review application code for credential handling paths from user input through transmission and storage. Check for credentials in URL parameters, query strings, HTTP headers, or application logs. Verify that credential storage uses approved cryptographic algorithms with appropriate salt and iteration counts. Test whether credentials can be extracted from memory dumps, core files, or crash reports.
weaknesses.remediation: Always transmit credentials over TLS 1.2+ encrypted channels. Store passwords using bcrypt with cost factor 12+, scrypt, or Argon2id. Never store credentials in reversible encryption or encoding. Remove credentials from URL parameters, logs, error messages, and debug output. Implement credential rotation policies for service accounts. Use platform-provided credential vaults (Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) for application secrets. Implement memory-safe credential handling that zeroes buffers after use.

weaknesses.title: Unprotected Transport of Credentials
weaknesses.description: The application transmits login credentials without using an encrypted channel, exposing them to network-level eavesdropping. When credentials are sent over unencrypted HTTP or other cleartext protocols, any attacker with access to the network path can intercept usernames and passwords through packet sniffing, man-in-the-middle attacks, or compromised network infrastructure. This is especially dangerous on shared networks such as public Wi-Fi, corporate LANs, and cloud environments where network isolation may not be guaranteed.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Use network scanning tools to identify authentication endpoints accessible over HTTP without TLS. Check for mixed content where login forms are served over HTTPS but submit credentials over HTTP. Verify HSTS headers are present and include subdomains. Test for SSL stripping attacks by downgrading connections and checking if the application falls back to HTTP. Review LDAP and other internal protocol configurations for use of STARTTLS or LDAPS rather than plaintext LDAP.
weaknesses.remediation: Enforce TLS 1.2+ on all authentication endpoints and credential transmission paths. Implement HSTS with a minimum max-age of 31536000 (one year) including subdomains and preload. Configure HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects at the load balancer/reverse proxy level. Use LDAPS (port 636) or STARTTLS instead of plaintext LDAP (port 389). Disable all plaintext protocol alternatives (FTP, Telnet, HTTP). Implement certificate pinning for mobile applications communicating authentication data.

weaknesses.title: Unverified Password Change
weaknesses.description: The product allows users to change their password without first requiring verification of the current password or equivalent identity proof. When password changes do not require re-authentication, an attacker who gains temporary access to an authenticated session (through session hijacking, CSRF, or physical access to an unlocked workstation) can change the password and lock out the legitimate user. This weakness bypasses the intent of authentication by allowing unauthorized password changes that persist beyond the compromised session.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test password change functionality by attempting to change the password without providing the current password. Check if the password change form includes a field for the current password and whether the server validates it. Verify that password changes through API endpoints also require re-authentication. Test whether administrative password reset flows properly verify the admin's identity before allowing changes to other users' passwords. Check for CSRF protection on password change endpoints.
weaknesses.remediation: Always require the current password or equivalent re-authentication (MFA challenge) before allowing password changes. Implement CSRF protection on all password change endpoints. After a password change, invalidate all existing sessions except the current one and send notification to all registered email addresses and phone numbers. Log all password change events with source IP, timestamp, and user agent for forensic purposes. Implement a cooldown period between consecutive password changes to prevent rapid credential cycling.

weaknesses.title: Weak Password Recovery Mechanism for Forgotten Password
weaknesses.description: The product contains a mechanism for recovering forgotten passwords that uses weak verification methods, enabling attackers to reset other users' passwords. Common weaknesses include security questions with easily researched answers, password reset tokens sent via insecure channels, predictable reset token generation, lack of rate limiting on reset requests, and overly informative error messages that confirm account existence. Password recovery mechanisms are frequent targets because they intentionally bypass normal authentication controls.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test the password reset flow for enumeration vulnerabilities by submitting known-valid and known-invalid email addresses and comparing responses. Analyze reset tokens for entropy and predictability by requesting multiple tokens and comparing patterns. Verify reset tokens expire after a short period (15-30 minutes) and are single-use. Check whether the reset endpoint is rate limited. Test if password reset links can be reused after the password has been changed. Verify that security questions, if used, are not easily guessable.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement password reset using high-entropy, single-use tokens (minimum 128 bits of randomness) sent to verified email addresses over TLS. Tokens must expire within 15-30 minutes. Return identical responses for valid and invalid email addresses to prevent enumeration. Rate limit reset requests to 3 per hour per account and 10 per hour per IP. Do not use security questions as the sole recovery mechanism. Implement MFA-based account recovery where possible. Log all password reset events and send notifications to the account holder.

weaknesses.title: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
weaknesses.description: The product contains hard-coded credentials such as passwords, API keys, or cryptographic keys embedded directly in source code, configuration files, or binary executables. Hard-coded credentials cannot be easily changed without modifying and redeploying the software, creating a persistent backdoor that persists across all installations. Once discovered through reverse engineering, code repository access, or public code leaks, these credentials compromise every instance of the software. This is one of the most commonly exploited weaknesses in IoT devices, embedded systems, and enterprise applications.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Run static analysis tools (Gitleaks, TruffleHog, Semgrep) to detect credentials in source code repositories including git history. Search code for patterns matching API keys, passwords, connection strings, and tokens using regular expressions. Review configuration files for hardcoded secrets. Scan compiled binaries with strings and entropy analysis tools. Check Docker images and container layers for embedded secrets. Review CI/CD pipeline configurations for exposed credentials.
weaknesses.remediation: Remove all hard-coded credentials and replace with runtime configuration from secure secret management systems (Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Implement secret scanning in CI/CD pipelines to prevent credential commits. Use environment variables or mounted secrets for application configuration. Rotate any credentials that were previously hard-coded as they must be assumed compromised. Implement pre-commit hooks (Gitleaks) to prevent accidental credential commits. For service-to-service authentication, use managed identities or workload identity federation instead of static credentials.

weaknesses.title: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort
weaknesses.description: The product stores password hashes using algorithms that do not require sufficient computational effort, enabling rapid offline password cracking. Fast hash algorithms like MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 (even with salting) can be brute-forced at billions of attempts per second using modern GPUs. Purpose-built password hashing functions like bcrypt, scrypt, and Argon2 are specifically designed to be computationally expensive and memory-hard, making offline cracking impractical. Using fast hash algorithms for password storage is a critical security failure that endangers all stored credentials.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review password storage code to identify the hashing algorithm used. Check for use of MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, or other fast cryptographic hash functions for password storage. Verify that bcrypt cost factor is at least 12, scrypt parameters meet OWASP recommendations, or Argon2id is used with appropriate memory and iteration parameters. Examine database schemas and stored hash formats to identify the algorithm (e.g., $2b$ prefix for bcrypt, $argon2id$ for Argon2). Check for unsalted hashes by comparing hash values across accounts.
weaknesses.remediation: Use Argon2id as the primary password hashing algorithm with minimum parameters: memory 19 MiB, iterations 2, parallelism 1 (per OWASP 2024 recommendations). Alternatively use bcrypt with cost factor 12+ or scrypt with N=2^17, r=8, p=1. Implement transparent hash migration: when users authenticate successfully against legacy hashes, re-hash their password with the current algorithm. Generate unique random salts (16+ bytes) per password. Never use MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, or other general-purpose hash functions for password storage. Benchmark hash computation to ensure it takes 100-500ms per hash.

weaknesses.title: Not Using Password Aging
weaknesses.description: The product does not require users to change their passwords periodically, potentially allowing compromised credentials to remain valid indefinitely. While NIST SP 800-63B no longer recommends forced periodic password rotation (as it leads to weaker password choices), password aging remains relevant for detecting credential compromise and ensuring that passwords eventually cycle. The nuanced position is that forced rotation should be replaced with event-driven password changes triggered by breach detection, suspicious activity, or credential compromise indicators.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review password policy configurations to determine if any password aging or expiration mechanisms exist. Check Active Directory Group Policy Objects for password age settings. Verify whether the application monitors breached credential databases and forces password changes when user credentials appear in breach dumps. Review whether there are mechanisms to force password changes after security incidents. Check if service account passwords have ever been rotated since creation.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement event-driven password changes rather than arbitrary time-based rotation: force password changes when credentials appear in breach databases (integrate with HIBP), after confirmed security incidents, and when suspicious account activity is detected. For service accounts that cannot use managed identities, implement automated rotation on 90-day cycles. Monitor breached credential databases continuously and notify users to change compromised passwords. Follow NIST SP 800-63B guidance: do not force periodic changes for end users but do enforce changes when compromise is suspected.

weaknesses.title: Password Aging with Long Expiration
weaknesses.description: The product uses password aging but sets the expiration period so long that compromised credentials could remain valid for an extended time before forced rotation occurs. If password expiration is set to 365 days or longer, an attacker who compromises credentials early in the cycle has nearly a full year of access before the password is forced to change. This weakness is compounded when there is no breach detection or anomalous access monitoring to trigger earlier password invalidation.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review password policy for maximum age settings. Check Active Directory GPO for MaxPasswordAge values exceeding 90 days. Identify accounts with passwords older than 180 days through directory queries. Verify that service accounts and privileged accounts have stricter rotation schedules. Check Azure AD/Entra ID password policies for cloud-managed accounts. Monitor for accounts where password age exceeds organizational policy limits.
weaknesses.remediation: If using time-based password expiration, set maximum password age to 90 days for privileged accounts and 180 days for standard accounts. Prefer event-driven password changes over calendar-based rotation per NIST SP 800-63B. Implement continuous monitoring for compromised credentials using breach intelligence feeds. Use Azure AD/Entra ID Password Protection to block known compromised passwords. Implement automated alerts for accounts with passwords exceeding age thresholds. Migrate service accounts to managed identities where possible to eliminate password aging concerns entirely.

weaknesses.title: Missing Password Field Masking
weaknesses.description: The product does not mask password input fields during entry, displaying the password in cleartext on screen and making it visible to shoulder surfers, screen recording malware, and screen-sharing software. This weakness is particularly concerning in shared workspace environments, during screen-sharing sessions, and when accessibility features like screen magnification are active. While 'show password' toggle functionality is acceptable, the default state should always be masked.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Inspect HTML form fields for password inputs to verify they use type='password' rather than type='text'. Check if custom JavaScript overrides the input type or renders password fields as plain text. Review mobile application forms for proper password masking in native input fields. Test whether 'show password' toggle defaults to masked state. Verify that password fields in administrative interfaces and configuration pages are also masked. Check browser developer tools to ensure autocomplete='new-password' or autocomplete='current-password' is set.
weaknesses.remediation: Set all password input fields to type='password' in HTML forms. If providing a 'show password' toggle, ensure the default state is masked and the toggle requires explicit user action. For mobile applications, use platform-native secure text entry components. Implement autocomplete attributes appropriately (autocomplete='current-password' for login, autocomplete='new-password' for registration/change). Prevent password fields from being captured by screen recording by using platform-specific secure input APIs where available. Disable clipboard paste interception on password fields.

weaknesses.title: Insufficient Session Expiration
weaknesses.description: The product does not sufficiently expire or invalidate session identifiers after a period of inactivity or after a reasonable maximum lifetime, allowing sessions to persist indefinitely. Long-lived sessions increase the window of opportunity for session hijacking, session fixation, and unauthorized access from shared or compromised devices. Without proper session expiration, a stolen session token remains valid until the user explicitly logs out, which may never occur on mobile devices or browser tabs left open.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review session configuration for idle timeout and absolute timeout values. Test whether sessions remain valid after extended periods of inactivity (>30 minutes for sensitive applications). Check if session tokens have a maximum lifetime regardless of activity. Verify that logout actually invalidates the server-side session and not just the client-side cookie. Test whether expired session tokens are properly rejected. Review OAuth2 token lifetimes and refresh token rotation policies.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement both idle timeout (15-30 minutes for sensitive applications) and absolute session lifetime (8-24 hours depending on risk). Store sessions server-side and validate on every request. Implement session invalidation on logout, password change, and privilege escalation. Use short-lived access tokens (5-15 minutes) with rotating refresh tokens in OAuth2/OIDC flows. Configure sliding session windows that extend with activity but enforce absolute maximums. Notify users before session expiration and provide graceful re-authentication.

weaknesses.title: Use of Password Hash Instead of Password for Authentication
weaknesses.description: The system authenticates users by comparing submitted password hashes rather than hashing the submitted password and comparing the result. This creates a pass-the-hash vulnerability where an attacker who obtains the stored hash can authenticate directly without knowing the plaintext password. The hash effectively becomes the credential, defeating the purpose of hashing. This is commonly seen in NTLM authentication, custom authentication schemes, and poorly designed API authentication.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review authentication code to determine whether the submitted credential is compared directly to the stored hash or whether the submitted plaintext is hashed before comparison. Check for pass-the-hash vulnerabilities in Windows/NTLM authentication. Analyze API authentication to determine if hash values are accepted as valid credentials. Test whether supplying a known hash value instead of a password results in successful authentication. Review network traffic for hash-based authentication protocols.
weaknesses.remediation: Always hash the user-submitted plaintext password server-side and compare the resulting hash against the stored hash. Never accept pre-hashed values from clients for authentication. Disable NTLM authentication in favor of Kerberos or modern protocols where possible. If NTLM must be used, implement additional controls such as restricting NTLM to specific applications, enabling NTLM audit logging, and deploying Credential Guard. For APIs, use token-based authentication (OAuth2/OIDC) rather than credential-based authentication.

weaknesses.title: Authentication Bypass by Capture-replay
weaknesses.description: The authentication scheme is vulnerable to replay attacks where an attacker captures valid authentication tokens, session identifiers, or protocol messages and retransmits them to gain unauthorized access. Replay attacks exploit authentication mechanisms that do not include sufficient freshness guarantees such as timestamps, nonces, or sequence numbers. This weakness affects both network-level authentication protocols and application-level token-based systems where tokens can be captured through network sniffing, log access, or browser history.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test authentication endpoints for replay vulnerability by capturing and resending valid authentication requests. Verify that authentication tokens include and validate timestamps, nonces, or one-time-use markers. Check OAuth2 authorization codes for single-use enforcement. Verify SAML assertions include NotOnOrAfter conditions and InResponseTo correlation. Test whether JWT tokens can be replayed after user logout. Monitor for duplicate authentication token usage in server logs.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement anti-replay mechanisms including: nonces (single-use random values) in authentication requests, strict timestamp validation with clock skew tolerance of 5 minutes maximum, one-time-use enforcement for authorization codes and SAML assertions, and token binding to client characteristics. Use TLS to prevent network-level token capture. Implement token rotation on use for refresh tokens. Include audience restrictions in SAML assertions and JWT tokens. Maintain a server-side token revocation list for invalidated tokens.

weaknesses.title: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
weaknesses.description: The product authenticates users based on information that can be spoofed or forged by an attacker, such as IP addresses, referrer headers, client-side cookies, or DNS hostnames. When authentication decisions rely on spoofable attributes rather than cryptographic proof of identity, attackers can forge these attributes to bypass authentication entirely. This weakness commonly manifests as IP-based access control lists used as the sole authentication mechanism, trust based on HTTP headers, or client-side authentication state management.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review authentication logic for decisions based on IP addresses, HTTP headers (X-Forwarded-For, Referer, Origin), or client-side state. Test whether modifying request headers bypasses authentication. Check if the application trusts proxy-injected headers without validation. Verify that client-side cookies or local storage are not the sole source of authentication state. Test whether DNS rebinding or host header manipulation affects authentication decisions.
weaknesses.remediation: Never use spoofable attributes (IP address, HTTP headers, client-side state) as the sole authentication mechanism. Implement cryptographic authentication using tokens signed by trusted identity providers. If IP-based restrictions are used, layer them as defense-in-depth alongside proper authentication, not as a replacement. Validate all trust-relevant headers at the edge (load balancer/reverse proxy) and strip client-supplied values. Use mutual TLS for service-to-service authentication where network-based identity is required.

weaknesses.title: Improper Certificate Validation
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly validate TLS/SSL certificates during authentication, allowing man-in-the-middle attacks that can intercept credentials and authentication tokens. Common failures include accepting self-signed certificates, ignoring certificate expiration, not checking certificate revocation status, accepting certificates with hostname mismatches, and implementing custom TrustManagers that blindly accept all certificates. This weakness is especially prevalent in mobile applications and service-to-service communication.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review TLS client code for disabled certificate validation, custom TrustManagers that accept all certificates, hostname verifier overrides, or certificate pinning bypass. Test with invalid, expired, self-signed, and hostname-mismatched certificates to verify proper rejection. Use network interception proxies (mitmproxy, Burp Suite) to test whether the application detects MITM attempts. Check mobile app code for common certificate validation bypass patterns. Review service mesh and internal service communication for missing mTLS validation.
weaknesses.remediation: Use platform-provided TLS libraries with default certificate validation enabled. Never disable hostname verification or implement custom TrustManagers that bypass validation. Implement certificate pinning for mobile applications and high-security service-to-service connections. Check certificate revocation using OCSP stapling or CRL distribution points. Use certificate transparency logs for monitoring. For internal services, implement mutual TLS with certificates from an internal CA with proper chain validation. Regularly audit TLS configuration using tools like SSL Labs or testssl.sh.

weaknesses.title: Incorrect Implementation of Authentication Algorithm
weaknesses.description: The product attempts to implement a standard authentication algorithm but does so incorrectly, introducing vulnerabilities that undermine the security properties the algorithm was designed to provide. Incorrect implementations include wrong comparison logic (timing-safe vs timing-unsafe), incorrect token generation algorithms, flawed challenge-response implementations, and improper state management in multi-step authentication flows. Even minor implementation errors in authentication algorithms can completely compromise their security.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review custom authentication code for correctness against the relevant specification (RFC, OWASP guidelines). Check for timing-unsafe string comparisons in credential verification using SAST rules. Verify that cryptographic operations use constant-time comparison functions. Test multi-step authentication flows for state management issues where steps can be skipped or reordered. Review token generation for use of cryptographically secure random number generators. Audit authentication error handling for logic that grants access on exception.
weaknesses.remediation: Use well-tested authentication libraries and frameworks rather than implementing authentication algorithms from scratch. When custom implementation is necessary, have the code reviewed by security specialists against the relevant specification. Use constant-time comparison functions for all credential and token verification (e.g., crypto.timingSafeEqual in Node.js, hmac.compare_digest in Python). Implement comprehensive test suites that verify both positive and negative authentication scenarios. Follow OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet for implementation guidance.

weaknesses.title: Missing Critical Step in Authentication
weaknesses.description: The product implements a multi-step authentication process but fails to enforce completion of all required steps, allowing users to skip critical verification stages. This weakness occurs when the authentication flow relies on client-side flow control rather than server-side state tracking, or when server-side validation does not verify that all prerequisite steps have been completed. Attackers can jump directly to the final authentication confirmation step, bypassing intermediate challenges such as MFA, CAPTCHA, or identity verification.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Map the complete multi-step authentication flow and test each step independently to verify it cannot be skipped. Attempt to access later steps directly without completing earlier steps. Check if the server tracks authentication state progression and validates that each step was completed in order. Test whether modifying client-side tokens or parameters allows skipping MFA challenges. Verify that the completion of each step is verified server-side before advancing to the next.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement server-side authentication state machines that track the current step for each authentication session and validate progression. Never rely on client-side flow control for authentication step enforcement. Use opaque server-side tokens that encode the current authentication state and are cryptographically bound to the session. Require explicit server-side verification that each prerequisite step was completed before allowing advancement. Implement session binding so that authentication state cannot be transferred between sessions.

weaknesses.title: Authentication Bypass by Primary Weakness
weaknesses.description: The product relies on a single authentication mechanism that can be completely bypassed due to a fundamental design flaw, leaving no secondary defense. Unlike CWE-304 where a step is missing from a multi-step process, this weakness describes situations where the primary authentication mechanism itself is fundamentally broken. Examples include authentication that can be bypassed by manipulating a request parameter, authentication gates that only check on the first request, and authentication that can be disabled via configuration accessible to unauthenticated users.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Perform comprehensive authentication bypass testing: manipulate authentication parameters, remove authentication headers, modify roles/tokens, and test direct object access. Check if authentication can be bypassed by changing HTTP methods (GET vs POST), adding/removing parameters, or modifying content types. Test whether authentication enforcement is consistent across all application layers. Review for authentication logic that defaults to granting access when an error or exception occurs.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement defense-in-depth authentication with multiple independent verification layers. Use proven authentication frameworks rather than custom implementations. Apply authentication enforcement at the framework level rather than per-endpoint. Implement a fail-closed authentication design where any error or exception results in access denial. Use automated security testing in CI/CD to continuously verify authentication enforcement. Conduct regular authentication architecture reviews and penetration testing.

weaknesses.title: Authentication Bypass Using an Alternate Path or Channel
weaknesses.description: The product requires authentication on the primary channel but exposes an alternate path or channel that does not enforce the same authentication requirements. Common examples include administrative interfaces accessible via different ports, API endpoints that bypass web application authentication, debug or maintenance channels, and mobile APIs that implement different authentication than the web application. Attackers enumerate these alternate paths to find the path of least resistance.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Enumerate all network-accessible endpoints, ports, and protocols to identify alternate access paths. Test whether the same resources can be accessed through different URL patterns, API versions, or network interfaces with different authentication requirements. Check for administrative interfaces on non-standard ports. Verify that mobile API, GraphQL, WebSocket, and legacy endpoints enforce the same authentication as the primary web interface. Scan for exposed management ports (JMX, SNMP, debug ports).
weaknesses.remediation: Implement a unified authentication layer that covers all access paths, protocols, and interfaces. Conduct thorough attack surface enumeration to identify all entry points. Apply the same authentication requirements regardless of the access channel. Close unnecessary network ports and management interfaces. Implement network segmentation to restrict access to administrative and debug interfaces. Use a reverse proxy or API gateway as a single point of authentication enforcement for all backend services. Regularly audit for new endpoints or services that may not be covered by authentication.

weaknesses.title: Reflection Attack in an Authentication Protocol
weaknesses.description: The authentication protocol is vulnerable to reflection attacks where an attacker uses the target's own challenge-response mechanism against it. In a reflection attack, the attacker opens two simultaneous sessions with the target, forwards the challenge from the first session as a challenge in the second session, and uses the target's response to the second challenge as the response to the first challenge. This is particularly relevant to mutual authentication protocols where both parties prove their identity using symmetric key operations.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review authentication protocol implementations for vulnerability to reflection attacks by analyzing whether challenges from the verifier can be reused as challenges to the verifier. Test by initiating concurrent authentication sessions and cross-using challenge-response pairs. Monitor for simultaneous authentication sessions from the same client with identical challenge values. Check whether the protocol includes role-specific challenge differentiation (e.g., different key derivation for initiator vs responder).
weaknesses.remediation: Implement role-differentiated challenges that are distinct for initiators and responders. Include session-specific nonces that bind challenges to particular protocol sessions. Use asymmetric authentication protocols where each party uses different keys for proving identity. Add sequence numbers or timestamps that prevent cross-session replay. For NTLM environments, implement Extended Protection for Authentication (EPA/Channel Binding) and migrate to Kerberos. Use standardized mutual authentication protocols (TLS mutual auth) rather than custom challenge-response schemes.

weaknesses.title: Authentication Bypass by Assumed-Immutable Data
weaknesses.description: The authentication mechanism relies on data values that the developer assumed could not be modified by an attacker, but which are actually under attacker control. This commonly manifests as authentication based on hidden form fields, client-side cookies, HTTP headers, or URL parameters that the developer incorrectly considers trustworthy. Any data transmitted from or through the client must be considered attacker-controlled and cannot serve as a reliable authentication factor without cryptographic integrity protection.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review authentication logic for reliance on client-modifiable data such as hidden form fields, cookies without integrity protection, URL parameters, or HTTP headers. Test by modifying these values to bypass or elevate authentication. Check for role or privilege information stored in unsigned cookies or local storage. Verify that JWT tokens are validated with proper signature verification and not just decoded. Test whether modifying client-side state changes the authentication or authorization outcome.
weaknesses.remediation: Never trust client-provided data for authentication decisions without cryptographic verification. Store authentication state server-side in secure session stores. Use signed and encrypted tokens (JWTs with RS256/ES256) when authentication state must traverse the client. Validate token signatures on every request using asymmetric keys. Implement server-side session management where the session ID is the only authentication-relevant value transmitted to the client. Apply the principle that any data from the client must be re-validated server-side.

weaknesses.title: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
weaknesses.description: The product does not sufficiently verify the origin or authenticity of data, allowing an attacker to inject or modify data that the system trusts. In the context of authentication, this includes accepting unsigned SAML assertions, unverified JWT tokens, spoofed identity provider responses, or user identity claims without cryptographic verification. This weakness is the root cause of many federation and SSO vulnerabilities where trust decisions are made based on data that has not been properly authenticated.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Verify that all SAML assertions are validated against the IdP's public key with proper XML signature verification including canonicalization. Check that JWT tokens are validated with proper algorithm specification (no 'alg: none' or symmetric/asymmetric confusion). Test whether unsigned or self-signed identity assertions are accepted. Verify that OAuth2 authorization server responses are validated. Check for XML Signature Wrapping vulnerabilities in SAML implementations. Test OIDC token validation including issuer, audience, and expiration checks.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement comprehensive cryptographic verification for all identity assertions: validate SAML assertion signatures with proper canonicalization and reference validation, validate JWT signatures with explicit algorithm specification (never allow 'none'), verify token issuer, audience, and expiration claims, and validate OAuth2 state parameters. Use well-tested libraries for SAML and JWT processing rather than custom parsers. Implement signature pinning for known identity providers. Reject any assertion that fails validation with no fallback to unsigned mode.

weaknesses.title: Origin Validation Error
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly verify the origin of an authentication-related request, allowing cross-origin attacks that can compromise user credentials or session state. In authentication contexts, this manifests as missing or improper CORS configuration allowing credential theft, absent CSRF protection on authentication endpoints, or improper validation of redirect URIs in OAuth2 flows. Origin validation failures can lead to cross-site request forgery on login/logout, OAuth2 authorization code theft, and cross-origin credential exfiltration.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test CORS configuration on authentication endpoints for overly permissive origins (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *) or reflection of arbitrary Origin headers. Verify CSRF protection on login, logout, password change, and MFA enrollment endpoints. Check OAuth2 redirect URI validation for open redirect vulnerabilities or insufficient pattern matching. Test SAML RelayState and destination validation. Verify that authentication cookies have SameSite attribute set appropriately.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement strict origin validation: configure CORS with explicit allowed origins (never use wildcard with credentials), implement CSRF tokens on all authentication state-changing endpoints, validate OAuth2 redirect URIs using exact string matching against a whitelist (no wildcards or pattern matching), set SameSite=Lax or SameSite=Strict on all authentication cookies, and validate SAML Destination and InResponseTo attributes. Use the state parameter in OAuth2 flows as a CSRF mitigation. Implement Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE) for all OAuth2 public clients.

weaknesses.title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) in Authentication Flows
weaknesses.description: The web application does not sufficiently protect authentication-related state-changing operations from cross-site request forgery, allowing an attacker to trick authenticated users into performing unintended authentication actions. CSRF on authentication endpoints can enable login CSRF (forcing the victim to authenticate as the attacker), logout CSRF (disrupting availability), password change CSRF (account takeover), and MFA enrollment/disenrollment CSRF. Authentication CSRF is particularly dangerous because it can be chained with other vulnerabilities for full account compromise.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test all authentication state-changing endpoints (login, logout, password change, MFA enrollment, account linking) for CSRF protection. Verify presence and validation of CSRF tokens. Check SameSite cookie attributes on session cookies. Test OAuth2 state parameter usage and validation. Attempt to forge authentication requests from cross-origin pages. Verify that CSRF protection cannot be bypassed by changing content types or request methods.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement CSRF protection on all authentication endpoints using synchronizer token pattern or double-submit cookie pattern. Set SameSite=Lax on all session cookies as a defense-in-depth measure. Validate the OAuth2 state parameter as a cryptographically random value bound to the user's session. Implement origin checking as an additional layer. Re-authenticate users before sensitive operations (password change, MFA changes). Use framework-provided CSRF protection rather than custom implementations.

weaknesses.title: Use of Insufficiently Random Values
weaknesses.description: The product uses insufficiently random numbers in a security context, making authentication tokens, session identifiers, password reset tokens, or cryptographic keys predictable. When random number generators with insufficient entropy are used for security-critical values, attackers can predict future values by observing a sequence of generated values or by exploiting known weaknesses in the random number generator. This directly undermines the security of session management, token-based authentication, and any cryptographic operations in the authentication system.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review code to identify all security-sensitive random value generation. Check whether cryptographically secure PRNGs are used (crypto.randomBytes in Node.js, secrets module in Python, SecureRandom in Java). Test generated values for randomness using statistical analysis tools. Collect multiple session tokens or authentication codes and analyze for patterns. Verify that seed values for PRNGs are not predictable (PID, timestamp, fixed values). Check for use of Math.random() or similar non-cryptographic generators in security contexts.
weaknesses.remediation: Use cryptographically secure random number generators for all security-sensitive values: Node.js crypto.randomBytes() or crypto.randomUUID(), Python secrets module, Java SecureRandom, .NET RNGCryptoServiceProvider. Generate session identifiers with at least 128 bits of entropy. Use UUIDv4 (not v1) for security tokens. Never use Math.random(), rand(), or other non-cryptographic PRNGs for security purposes. Ensure the CSPRNG is properly seeded from the operating system's entropy source (/dev/urandom on Linux, BCryptGenRandom on Windows).

weaknesses.title: Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly verify cryptographic signatures on authentication tokens, assertions, or protocol messages, allowing attackers to forge authentication data. This includes failing to verify JWT signatures, accepting SAML assertions with invalid signatures, ignoring signature verification failures, or using improper signature verification algorithms. Signature verification failures in authentication contexts directly lead to authentication bypass as attackers can craft arbitrary identity claims.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test JWT validation by submitting tokens with 'alg: none', tokens signed with different keys, and tokens with modified payloads but unchanged signatures. Test SAML assertion processing with unsigned assertions, assertions signed with different keys, and XML Signature Wrapping attacks. Verify that the application rejects tokens where signature verification fails rather than falling back to unsigned mode. Check for algorithm confusion attacks (RS256 vs HS256) in JWT validation.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement strict signature verification using well-tested libraries (jose for Node.js, PyJWT for Python, Nimbus JOSE for Java). Explicitly specify allowed signing algorithms and reject tokens with unexpected algorithms. For JWT: never allow 'alg: none', use RS256 or ES256 with proper key management, verify issuer and audience claims. For SAML: validate XML signatures with proper canonicalization, verify certificate chain to trusted CA, implement signature wrapping protection. Pin expected signing keys for known identity providers.
